🟡 Resilience Earth

Bioregional Organizer
🌐 Visit website

Resilience Earth is a nonprofit worker cooperative based in Catalonia, Spain, dedicated to transformative change through community resilience, regenerative design, democratic innovation and the social and solidarity economy.

Bioregional Context

Located in Northern Catalonia, La Garrotxa encompasses 21 municipalities with approximately 60,000 residents. It's a mountainous comarca best known for its dramatic volcanic landscape, featuring over 40 dormant volcanic cones, lush forests, and fertile valleys.

Place
Olot, Catalonia, Spain
links
https://youtu.be/VEORXmWyKkE

Tags

🪢 Network_Weaving 📐 Participatory_Planning_and_Strategies 🔘 Governance_Bodies 📊 Socioeconomic_Indicators 🎉 Congressing_and_Events 🎒 Learning_Programs

Background


Located in Northern Catalonia, La Garrotxa encompasses 21 municipalities with approximately 60,000 residents. It's a mountainous comarca (county) best known for its dramatic volcanic landscape. The region features over 40 dormant volcanic cones, lush forests, and fertile valleys shaped by ancient lava flows. La Garrotxa has a strong tradition of environmental stewardship, sustainable tourism, and local food systems.

Origins and Network


Resilience Earth started eight years ago when two co-founders, Erika and Oscar, began working in La Garrotxa. Today, they're a nonprofit worker cooperative of 14 people, but their approach remains fundamentally the same: working from the edge, focusing on centers rather than boundaries, building theory from practice rather than applying frameworks.

They've discovered bioregioning through doing. As Oscar reflects, "We're going really bottom up. We're not following a lot of the theoretical framework—we're kind of building one little by little as we evolve and as we work more on our territories."

Philosophy & Approach


After seven years of practice, their most useful tool became “landscape units”—a framework developed by Joan M. at the University of Girona. La Garrotxa has 2.5 landscape units, each usually spanning four to six municipalities with shared characteristics. Units can act as centers because municipalities within them are tightly interlinked. River work might span seven or eight units; ecosystem work gathers the units around a feature. The frame flexes with strategy and goal.

They focus on managing interconnectivity. From that angle, boundaries create more problems than clarity; what counts are centers—places where relationships and coherence emerge.

They set out to root change in one place—under their feet in La Garrotxa—and to convene actors around a shared aim rather than lead alone.

Activities

🔸

Beuda Democratic Innovation Project

 🔸

La Garrotxa 2030 Strategic Planning Initiative

 🔸

Democracy Festival (Festival Iltir)

 🔸

Local Government Incubator (INCAT)

Vision and Legacy


Resilience Earth brings more than eight years of practice and a theoretical model refined in use. Two years ago the Barcelona provincial government asked them to define territorial resilience; they used the commission to consolidate learnings into a framework that continues to guide the work.

Their through-line is resilience thinking, interconnectivity, and centers over boundaries. They have shown democracy shifting in small towns, strategy emerging from broad participation, and—when governance is truly shared—politics edging toward service rather than capture.

Their experience suggests bioregional governance can change how people relate to place, how decisions are made, and how communities coordinate from the smallest municipality to the full bioregion—democracy practiced by the people who live there.
